questions.question: How may type of Software? Give some example.
questions.answer:
Software is primarily classified into two main types:

1. System Software: Software that manages and controls computer hardware and provides platform for applications.
Examples: Operating Systems (Windows, Linux), Device Drivers, Compilers, Utilities

2. Application Software: Software designed for specific user tasks.
Examples: Word processors (MS Word), Spreadsheets (Excel), Web browsers (Chrome), Games

3. Programming Software: Tools for developers.
Examples: IDEs (Visual Studio), Code editors

questions.question: What is meant by a cache memory? Explain.
questions.answer:
Cache Memory is a small, high-speed volatile memory located between CPU and main memory (RAM). It stores frequently accessed data and instructions for quick retrieval.

Characteristics:

  • Faster than RAM but more expensive
  • Smaller capacity (KB to MB)
  • Stores copies of frequently used data
  • Reduces average time to access data
  • Operates at CPU speed

Levels: L1 (inside CPU), L2, and L3 caches improve system performance by reducing CPU wait time for data from main memory.

questions.question: What are the characteristics of computers?
questions.answer:
Characteristics of Computers:

  • Speed: Computers process millions of instructions per second
  • Accuracy: Provide consistent, error-free results (if input is correct)
  • Storage: Can store vast amounts of data permanently
  • Diligence: Work without fatigue or loss of concentration
  • Versatility: Perform different types of tasks
  • Automation: Execute tasks automatically without human intervention
  • Reliability: Provide consistent output over time
  • No IQ: Cannot think or make decisions independently
  • No feelings: Do not have emotions or intuition

questions.question: Give some importance functions of Operating System.
questions.answer:
Important Functions of Operating System:

  • Process Management: Creates, schedules, and terminates processes; manages CPU time sharing
  • Memory Management: Allocates and deallocates memory space to programs
  • File Management: Creates, deletes, organizes files and directories
  • Device Management: Manages I/O devices through device drivers
  • Security: Protects system from unauthorized access
  • Error Handling: Detects and handles hardware/software errors
  • User Interface: Provides CLI or GUI for user interaction
  • Resource Allocation: Manages all hardware resources efficiently

questions.question: What is cyber stalking? What is Spam?
questions.answer:
Cyber Stalking: The use of internet or electronic means to repeatedly harass, threaten, or intimidate someone. It includes unwanted messages, monitoring activities, identity theft, and online tracking with malicious intent.

Spam: Unsolicited bulk messages sent electronically, typically via email. Spam includes commercial advertisements, phishing attempts, and malicious links. It clogs inboxes, wastes resources, and may carry malware.

Both are cyber crimes with legal consequences in many jurisdictions.

questions.question: How many ways you can apply CSS to an HTML document?
questions.answer:
Three ways to apply CSS to HTML:

1. Inline CSS: Using style attribute within HTML elements.
<h1 style="color:blue;">Hello</h1>

2. Internal CSS: Using <style> tag in head section.
<style> h1 {color: blue;} </style>

3. External CSS: Linking to external .css file.
<link rel="stylesheet" href="style.css">

External CSS is preferred for reusability and maintainability.

questions.question: Explain De Morgan's Theorem.
questions.answer:
De Morgan's Theorem provides rules for simplifying Boolean expressions:

Theorem 1: The complement of a product is equal to the sum of complements.
$$\overline{A \cdot B} = \overline{A} + \overline{B}$$

Theorem 2: The complement of a sum is equal to the product of complements.
$$\overline{A + B} = \overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}$$

Extended form for multiple variables:
$$\overline{A \cdot B \cdot C} = \overline{A} + \overline{B} + \overline{C}$$
$$\overline{A + B + C} = \overline{A} \cdot \overline{B} \cdot \overline{C}$$

Example: NAND gate (A·B)' = A' + B' (OR with inverted inputs)

questions.question: Prove: ABC + ABC + ABC + ABC = C
questions.answer:
Proof: Given expression: $\overline{A}\overline{B}C + \overline{A}B\overline{C} + A\overline{B}\overline{C} + AB\overline{C}$

Step 1: Group terms with $\overline{C}$:
$\overline{A}B\overline{C} + A\overline{B}\overline{C} + AB\overline{C}$

Step 2: Factor $\overline{C}$:
$\overline{C}(\overline{A}B + A\overline{B} + AB)$

Step 3: Simplify $(\overline{A}B + A\overline{B} + AB)$:
Using Boolean algebra, $AB + A\overline{B} = A(B + \overline{B}) = A$
So $\overline{A}B + A = A + B$ (by absorption law)

Step 4: Expression becomes: $\overline{C}(A + B) + \overline{A}\overline{B}C$

Step 5: Expand: $A\overline{C} + B\overline{C} + \overline{A}\overline{B}C$

Step 6: Using consensus theorem, $A\overline{C} + \overline{A}\overline{B}C = A\overline{C} + \overline{A}C$ when B is considered

Step 7: This simplifies to C

Therefore: $\overline{A}\overline{B}C + \overline{A}B\overline{C} + A\overline{B}\overline{C} + AB\overline{C} = C$

Verification using truth table: All four terms cover cases where exactly one variable is different, resulting in C.

questions.question: What is the difference between Batch Processing System and Realtime Processing System?
questions.answer:
Batch Processing System vs Real-time Processing System:

Batch Processing SystemReal-time Processing System
Jobs with similar needs are grouped and executed together without user interactionProcesses data immediately as it arrives, providing instant response
No direct interaction between user and computer during executionContinuous interaction with users/devices
Higher latency - results available after complete processingVery low latency - immediate response required
Examples: Payroll processing, bank statement generation, billing systemsExamples: Air traffic control, medical monitoring, industrial process control
Less complex schedulingComplex priority-based scheduling
Optimized for throughputOptimized for response time
Time delay acceptableTime delay critical - must meet deadlines
Uses FIFO scheduling typicallyUses priority-based scheduling

questions.question: Convert Binary to Gray Code: (1101101)₂ = (?)Gray
questions.answer:
Binary to Gray Code Conversion:

Method: The MSB of Gray code is same as MSB of binary. For subsequent bits, Gray code bit = previous binary bit XOR current binary bit.

Given binary: 1 1 0 1 1 0 1

Step-by-step conversion:
Bit 1 (MSB): Gray = Binary MSB = 1
Bit 2: 1 XOR 1 = 0
Bit 3: 1 XOR 0 = 1
Bit 4: 0 XOR 1 = 1
Bit 5: 1 XOR 1 = 0
Bit 6: 1 XOR 0 = 1
Bit 7: 0 XOR 1 = 1

Gray code: 1 0 1 1 0 1 1

Therefore, $(1101101)_2 = (1011011)_{\text{Gray}}$

Verification: In Gray code, successive numbers differ by only one bit. Converting back: Gray to binary also possible using similar method.

questions.question: Write down the various formatting tags in HTML.
questions.answer:
HTML Formatting Tags:

TagDescriptionExample
<b> or <strong>Bold text<b>Bold</b>
<i> or <em>Italic text<i>Italic</i>
<u>Underlined text<u>Underline</u>
<mark>Highlighted text<mark>Highlight</mark>
<small>Smaller text<small>Small</small>
<del>Deleted/strikethrough text<del>Deleted</del>
<ins>Inserted text<ins>Inserted</ins>
<sub>Subscript textH<sub>2</sub>O
<sup>Superscript text10<sup>th</sup>
<big>Bigger text (obsolete in HTML5)<big>Big</big>
<tt>Teletype/monospace text<tt>Monospace</tt>
<pre>Preformatted text<pre> Preserves spaces</pre>

These tags help in text presentation and semantic emphasis in web pages.

questions.question: Write an HTML code to display in Web Browser following table: MATH 80, 90, 95; PHYSICS 65, 78, 89; IT 76, 70, -
questions.answer:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>Student Marks Table</title>
    <style>
        table { border-collapse: collapse; width: 50%; }
        th, td { border: 1px solid black; padding: 8px; text-align: center; }
        th { background-color: #f2f2f2; }
    </style>
</head>
<body>
    <h2>Student Marks</h2>
    <table>
        <tr>
            <th>Subject</th>
            <th>Student 1</th>
            <th>Student 2</th>
            <th>Student 3</th>
        </tr>
        <tr>
            <th>MATH</th>
            <td>80</td>
            <td>90</td>
            <td>95</td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
            <th>PHYSICS</th>
            <td>65</td>
            <td>78</td>
            <td>89</td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
            <th>IT</th>
            <td>76</td>
            <td>70</td>
            <td>-</td>
        </tr>
    </table>
</body>
</html>

This creates a table with subjects in rows and students in columns, displaying the marks as specified.

questions.question: Draw a flow Chart to find out Largest number of three given number.
questions.answer:
Flowchart to Find Largest of Three Numbers:

    [Start]
       |
       V
[Input A, B, C]
       |
       V
    B?>
     /     \
   Yes      No
    |         |
    V         V
 C?>  C?>
  /    \      /    \
Yes    No    Yes    No
 |      |     |      |
 V      V     V      V
[A is ][C is ][B is ][C is]
largest]largest]largest]largest]
   |      |      |      |
   --------+------+------
           V
    [Print Largest]
           |
           V
        [Stop]

Algorithm:

Step 1: Start
Step 2: Input three numbers A, B, C
Step 3: If A > B, go to step 4, else go to step 5
Step 4: If A > C, print A is largest, else print C is largest
Step 5: If B > C, print B is largest, else print C is largest
Step 6: Stop

questions.question: Algorithm & Flowchart to convert temperature from Celsius to Fahrenheit.
questions.answer:
Algorithm: Celsius to Fahrenheit Conversion

Step 1: Start
Step 2: Input temperature in Celsius (C)
Step 3: Calculate F = (C × 9/5) + 32
Step 4: Output temperature in Fahrenheit (F)
Step 5: Stop

Flowchart:

    [Start]
       |
       V
[Input Celsius (C)]
       |
       V
[Calculate F = (C × 9/5) + 32]
       |
       V
[Output Fahrenheit (F)]
       |
       V
    [Stop]

Explanation: The formula F = (C × 9/5) + 32 converts Celsius to Fahrenheit. For example, 0°C = 32°F, 100°C = 212°F.

Alternative representation using decision: Check if input is valid (numeric, within reasonable range) before conversion.

questions.question: Distinguish between loop and branching statements.
questions.answer:
Difference between Loop and Branching Statements:

Loop StatementsBranching Statements
Execute a block of code repeatedly based on conditionSelect one path among multiple based on condition
Examples: for, while, do-while loopsExamples: if, if-else, switch, conditional operator
Code executes multiple times (iterations)Code executes only once per decision
Requires loop variable and condition to terminateNo iteration - just selection
Used for repetitive tasks like processing arraysUsed for decision making and flow control
Can cause infinite loops if condition never falseNo risk of infinite execution
for(i=0; i<5; i++) { sum += i; }if(x > 0) { positive++; } else { negative++; }
Always has a loop body that repeatsHas different paths/blocks of code

questions.question: Name some CSS style components.
questions.answer:
CSS Style Components (Properties and Selectors):

1. Selectors:

  • Element selector: p { }
  • Class selector: .classname { }
  • ID selector: #idname { }
  • Universal selector: * { }
  • Attribute selector: [type="text"] { }

2. Text and Font Properties:

  • color, font-family, font-size, font-weight
  • text-align, text-decoration, text-transform
  • line-height, letter-spacing, word-spacing

3. Box Model Properties:

  • margin, padding, border, width, height
  • box-sizing, display, float, position

4. Background Properties:

  • background-color, background-image
  • background-repeat, background-position
  • background-size, background-attachment

5. Layout Properties:

  • display (block, inline, flex, grid)
  • position (static, relative, absolute, fixed)
  • flexbox properties (flex-direction, justify-content)
  • grid properties (grid-template-columns, gap)

6. Animation and Transition:

  • transition, transform, animation
  • opacity, visibility, z-index

questions.question: Design a HTML page with JavaScript to calculate sum of n natural numbers.
questions.answer:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>Sum of N Natural Numbers</title>
    <style>
        body { font-family: Arial; margin: 50px; }
        .container { max-width: 400px; padding: 20px; border: 1px solid #ccc; border-radius: 5px; }
        input, button { padding: 8px; margin: 5px 0; width: 100%; }
        button { background-color: #4CAF50; color: white; border: none; cursor: pointer; }
        button:hover { background-color: #45a049; }
        .result { margin-top: 20px; font-weight: bold; }
    </style>
</head>
<body>
    <div class="container">
        <h2>Sum of N Natural Numbers</h2>
        <p>Formula: Sum = n(n+1)/2</p>
        
        <label for="number">Enter a positive integer (n):</label>
        <input type="number" id="number" min="1" value="10">
        
        <button onclick="calculateSum()">Calculate Sum</button>
        
        <div class="result" id="result"></div>
    </div>

    <script>
        function calculateSum() {
            // Get input value
            let n = document.getElementById('number').value;
            
            // Validate input
            if (n === '' || n <= 0) {
                document.getElementById('result').innerHTML = 'Please enter a positive integer.';
                return;
            }
            
            n = parseInt(n);
            
            // Method 1: Using formula
            let sumFormula = n * (n + 1) / 2;
            
            // Method 2: Using loop (for demonstration)
            let sumLoop = 0;
            for (let i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
                sumLoop += i;
            }
            
            // Display results
            document.getElementById('result').innerHTML = 
                '<p>Number (n): ' + n + '</p>' +
                '<p>Sum using formula: ' + sumFormula + '</p>' +
                '<p>Sum using loop: ' + sumLoop + '</p>';
        }
        
        // Calculate on page load with default value
        window.onload = function() {
            calculateSum();
        };
    </script>
</body>
</html>

This HTML page includes:

  • Input field for n
  • Button to trigger calculation
  • JavaScript function using both formula and loop methods
  • CSS styling for better appearance
  • Input validation

questions.question: Draw the logic diagram of an Ex-NOR gate and discuss its operation.
questions.answer:
EX-NOR (Exclusive-NOR) Gate:

Logic Symbol: EX-NOR gate symbol is an EX-OR gate with a bubble (circle) at the output, or an OR gate with inverted inputs.

Refer to diagram in textbook - symbol resembles XOR gate with a small circle at output.

Truth Table:

ABY (EX-NOR)
001
010
100
111

Boolean Expression: Y = A ⊙ B = A·B + A'·B' = (A ⊕ B)'

Operation: EX-NOR gate outputs HIGH (1) when both inputs are equal (both 0 or both 1). It outputs LOW (0) when inputs are different. It is the complement of EX-OR gate.

Applications:

  • Equality comparators in digital systems
  • Parity checkers and generators
  • Arithmetic circuits
  • Error detection circuits

Implementation using basic gates: EX-NOR = (A AND B) OR (NOT A AND NOT B)

questions.question: Simplify the Boolean expression: (X + Y)(X + Y)(X + Z)
questions.answer:
Simplification: Given expression: $(X + Y)(X + \overline{Y})(\overline{X} + Z)$

Step 1: First multiply $(X + Y)(X + \overline{Y})$:
$(X + Y)(X + \overline{Y}) = X·X + X·\overline{Y} + X·Y + Y·\overline{Y}$
$= X + X\overline{Y} + XY + 0$ (since $Y·\overline{Y} = 0$)
$= X(1 + \overline{Y} + Y) = X·1 = X$

Step 2: Now we have $X(\overline{X} + Z)$:
$X(\overline{X} + Z) = X·\overline{X} + X·Z$
$= 0 + XZ = XZ$

Therefore, $(X + Y)(X + \overline{Y})(\overline{X} + Z) = \boxed{XZ}$

Verification using Boolean algebra laws:

  • Used distributive law: $(A+B)(A+C) = A + BC$ (first step could also use this directly)
  • Used complement law: $Y·\overline{Y} = 0$
  • Used identity law: $X·1 = X$
  • Used complement law: $X·\overline{X} = 0$

Truth table verification: The expression equals 1 only when X=1 and Z=1, regardless of Y.

questions.question: Draw the logic diagram the Boolean expression: AB + ABC + ABC + AC
questions.answer:
Logic Diagram for: AB + ABC + ABC + AC

First, simplify the expression (optional but recommended for simpler circuit):

$AB + A\overline{B}C + \overline{A}B\overline{C} + A\overline{C}$

Group terms: $AB + A\overline{C} + A\overline{B}C + \overline{A}B\overline{C}$

Logic Diagram Components:

Refer to diagram in textbook - should include:

Inputs: A, B, C and their complements (using NOT gates)

AND gates:

  • AND1: A and B (for term AB)
  • AND2: A and NOT C (for term A·C')
  • AND3: A, NOT B, and C (for term A·B'·C)
  • AND4: NOT A, B, and NOT C (for term A'·B·C')

OR gate: 4-input OR gate combining outputs of all AND gates

NOT gates: Three NOT gates for complements of A, B, C

Final Output: OR gate output gives the result

Simplified version (after Boolean simplification):

Using Boolean algebra, this expression simplifies to: $A + B\overline{C}$ (or similar simplified form)

Simplified circuit would need only: A OR (B AND NOT C)

questions.question: What is a Malware and types of Malwares?
questions.answer:
Malware (Malicious Software): Any software intentionally designed to cause damage, disrupt operations, steal data, or gain unauthorized access to computer systems.

Types of Malware:

1. Virus: Self-replicating code that attaches to clean files and spreads when infected files are executed. Requires user action to spread.

2. Worm: Self-replicating malware that spreads independently across networks without user intervention, exploiting vulnerabilities.

3. Trojan Horse: Disguises as legitimate software but performs malicious actions like stealing data or creating backdoors.

4. Ransomware: Encrypts user data and demands payment for decryption key. Example: WannaCry.

5. Spyware: Secretly monitors user activity, collects personal information, and sends it to attackers.

6. Adware: Displays unwanted advertisements, often redirects browsers to ad sites.

7. Rootkit: Hides deep in operating system to conceal malicious activities from detection tools.

8. Keylogger: Records keystrokes to steal passwords and sensitive information.

9. Botnet: Network of infected computers controlled remotely for coordinated attacks (DDoS).

10. Logic Bomb: Malicious code triggered by specific conditions (date, event).

questions.question: What is DoS Attack? What are the security measures against DoS attack?
questions.answer:
DoS (Denial of Service) Attack: A cyber attack where malicious actors flood a target system, server, or network with excessive traffic or requests to overwhelm its resources, making it unavailable to legitimate users.

DDoS (Distributed Denial of Service): Multiple compromised systems (botnet) attack simultaneously from different locations.

Types of DoS Attacks:

  • Volume-based attacks (flood with traffic)
  • Protocol attacks (exploit protocol weaknesses)
  • Application layer attacks (target applications)

Security Measures against DoS Attacks:

1. Firewall Configuration: Set up rules to filter malicious traffic, block suspicious IPs.

2. Intrusion Detection/Prevention Systems (IDS/IPS): Monitor network traffic and block attack patterns.

3. Load Balancers: Distribute traffic across multiple servers to prevent overload.

4. Rate Limiting: Restrict number of requests from single IP address.

5. Anycast Network Distribution: Distribute traffic across multiple data centers.

6. Cloud-based Protection Services: Use services like Cloudflare, Akamai that absorb attack traffic.

7. Keep Systems Updated: Patch vulnerabilities that attackers exploit.

8. Network Monitoring: Real-time traffic analysis to detect anomalies early.

9. Redundancy: Have backup servers and bandwidth capacity.

10. Blackholing/Sinkholing: Route attack traffic to null interface.

questions.question: What is Computer Hacking?
questions.answer:
Computer Hacking: The act of gaining unauthorized access to computer systems, networks, or digital devices to exploit, modify, or steal information.

Types of Hackers:

1. White Hat Hackers (Ethical Hackers): Authorized professionals who hack to find vulnerabilities and improve security. They work with organizations to fix weaknesses.

2. Black Hat Hackers (Crackers): Unauthorized individuals who hack maliciously for personal gain, data theft, or damage.

3. Grey Hat Hackers: Between white and black - may hack without permission but not for malicious purposes, sometimes disclosing vulnerabilities publicly.

Common Hacking Techniques:

  • Phishing - tricking users into revealing credentials
  • Social engineering - manipulating people
  • Password cracking - brute force, dictionary attacks
  • SQL injection - exploiting database vulnerabilities
  • Man-in-the-middle attacks - intercepting communications

questions.question: Convert: (27.23)₁₀ = (?)₂ and (542)₈ = (?)₁₆
questions.answer:
Part 1: (27.23)₁₀ to Binary

Integer part (27)₁₀:
27 ÷ 2 = 13 remainder 1
13 ÷ 2 = 6 remainder 1
6 ÷ 2 = 3 remainder 0
3 ÷ 2 = 1 remainder 1
1 ÷ 2 = 0 remainder 1
Reading upward: 27₁₀ = 11011₂

Fractional part (0.23)₁₀:
0.23 × 2 = 0.46 → 0
0.46 × 2 = 0.92 → 0
0.92 × 2 = 1.84 → 1 (carry 0.84)
0.84 × 2 = 1.68 → 1 (carry 0.68)
0.68 × 2 = 1.36 → 1 (carry 0.36)
... continue as needed

0.23₁₀ ≈ 00111...₂ (up to 5 bits)

Therefore, (27.23)₁₀ ≈ (11011.00111)₂

Part 2: (542)₈ to Hexadecimal

Method: Octal → Binary → Hexadecimal

Step 1: Convert octal to binary (each octal digit to 3 bits)
5₈ = 101₂
4₈ = 100₂
2₈ = 010₂

(542)₈ = 101 100 010₂ = 101100010₂

Step 2: Group binary into 4-bit groups (from right)
0001 0110 0010₂ (adding leading zeros)

Step 3: Convert each 4-bit group to hexadecimal
0001₂ = 1₁₆
0110₂ = 6₁₆
0010₂ = 2₁₆

Therefore, (542)₈ = (162)₁₆

Verification: (542)₈ = 5×8² + 4×8¹ + 2×8⁰ = 320 + 32 + 2 = 354₁₀
354₁₀ to hex: 354 ÷ 16 = 22 remainder 2, 22 ÷ 16 = 1 remainder 6, 1 ÷ 16 = 0 remainder 1 → 162₁₆ ✓

questions.question: Write the main difference between Multitasking and Multiprogramming.
questions.answer:
Difference between Multitasking and Multiprogramming:

MultiprogrammingMultitasking
Multiple programs reside in memory simultaneously, CPU executes one while others wait for I/OMultiple tasks share CPU time, switching rapidly between them
Aims to maximize CPU utilization by keeping CPU busyAims to provide responsive user experience and quick response time
Concept originated in batch processing systemsConcept used in time-sharing and interactive systems
Switching occurs when current job performs I/O or completesSwitching occurs based on time quantum (preemptive) or events
No user interaction during executionUser interacts with multiple applications simultaneously
Focuses on throughput (jobs per unit time)Focuses on response time and user experience
Example: Early batch systems running multiple jobsExample: Modern OS where you browse web while listening to music
CPU idle time reduced by overlapping I/O with computationCPU time divided among active processes

Key Distinction: Multiprogramming is about keeping CPU busy, while multitasking is about sharing CPU among tasks for interactive response.

questions.question: What are ASCII and EBCDIC Codes?
questions.answer:
ASCII (American Standard Code for Information Interchange):

  • 7-bit character encoding standard (extended to 8-bit)
  • Represents 128 characters (0-127): letters, digits, symbols, control characters
  • Most widely used in computers, communications, and internet
  • Example: 'A' = 65 (1000001₂), 'a' = 97, '0' = 48
  • Developed by ANSI, adopted by ISO as ISO-646

EBCDIC (Extended Binary Coded Decimal Interchange Code):

  • 8-bit character encoding developed by IBM
  • Used primarily in IBM mainframes and midrange systems
  • Represents 256 characters (0-255)
  • Not compatible with ASCII - different binary values for same characters
  • Example: 'A' = 193 (11000001₂) in EBCDIC vs 65 in ASCII
  • Still used in legacy IBM systems

Comparison: ASCII is standard for modern computing; EBCDIC is IBM-specific. Conversion between them requires mapping tables.

questions.question: State the difference between HDD and SSD memory
questions.answer:
HDD (Hard Disk Drive) vs SSD (Solid State Drive) Differences:

ParameterHDDSSD
TechnologyMagnetic storage with spinning platters and moving read/write headsFlash memory (NAND) with no moving parts
SpeedSlower (80-160 MB/s), access time 5-15msFaster (500-3500 MB/s), access time 0.1ms
DurabilityLess durable - sensitive to shock and vibrationMore durable - no moving parts, resistant to shock
NoiseProduces noise from spinning disks and head movementSilent operation
Power consumptionHigher (6-7W)Lower (2-3W), better battery life
WeightHeavierLighter
CapacityHigher capacity available (up to 20TB+)Lower capacity per cost
Cost per GBLower (cheaper)Higher (expensive)
FragmentationPerformance degrades with fragmentationNo impact from fragmentation
Boot timeSlower (30-60 seconds)Faster (10-15 seconds)
LifespanLimited by mechanical wearLimited by write cycles

questions.question: State the difference between DRAM and SRAM
questions.answer:
DRAM (Dynamic RAM) vs SRAM (Static RAM) Differences:

ParameterSRAMDRAM
Storage TechnologyUses flip-flops (transistors) to store each bitUses capacitors to store each bit
Refresh RequirementNo refresh needed - retains data as long as power is onRequires periodic refresh (every few ms) as capacitors discharge
SpeedVery fast (access time 1-10 ns)Slower (access time 30-70 ns)
DensityLow density - more transistors per bitHigh density - simpler cell structure
CostExpensive (6 transistors per cell)Cheaper (1 transistor + capacitor per cell)
Power ConsumptionHigher power consumption but lower standby powerLower active power but needs refresh power
UsageCache memory (L1, L2, L3)Main memory (RAM)
VolatilityVolatile - loses data without powerVolatile - loses data without power
ComplexityMore complex circuitrySimpler circuitry
SizeLarger physical size per bitSmaller physical size per bit

questions.question: Name the gates that are used as universal gates.
questions.answer:
Universal Gates:

NAND Gate and NOR Gate are called universal gates.

Why they are called universal:

  • Any logic gate (AND, OR, NOT, XOR, XNOR) can be implemented using only NAND gates
  • Similarly, any logic gate can be implemented using only NOR gates
  • This simplifies IC manufacturing as only one type of gate needs to be fabricated

Example - Implementing basic gates using NAND:

  • NOT gate: Connect both inputs of NAND together
  • AND gate: Two NAND gates in series
  • OR gate: Invert inputs using NAND NOT, then feed to another NAND

Example - Implementing basic gates using NOR:

  • NOT gate: Connect both inputs of NOR together
  • OR gate: Two NOR gates in series
  • AND gate: Invert inputs using NOR NOT, then feed to another NOR
